nodes.description: A trajectory that uses lunar gravity to curve the spacecraft's path and return it to Earth without additional propulsive burns. Safety net — if main engine fails, crew comes home.
O2O laser link achieving 260 Mbps at lunar distance (384,400 km). 100mm telescope with gimbals. 130x improvement over S-band radio. First crewed deep space test.
AVCOAT ablative heat shield absorbs reentry heat by charring and ablating. Modified billet loading for Artemis II addresses Artemis I char loss. Direct entry replaces skip profile.
23.5-hour orbit at 70,000 km apogee. Crew and vehicle checkout in the Van Allen radiation belts before committing to TLI. One orbit = one full systems check.
AVATAR investigation: microfluidic chips mimicking astronaut organs. First test beyond LEO and Van Allen belts. Measures radiation + microgravity effects on human tissue.
Mathematical modeling of how elastic surfaces vibrate under stress. Germain's 1816 prize-winning work. Directly applicable to spacecraft structural analysis and heat shield behavior.
Falling barometric pressure triggers increased foraging activity in birds. The pressure change precedes the storm — animals predict weather from data humans need instruments to measure.
Model of synchronization in biological systems. Fireflies adjust flash timing toward neighbors, producing collective synchronization from individual phase coupling. Classic emergence.

MAScOT terminal on Orion achieves 260 Mbps downlink at lunar distance via 1550 nm laser to ground stations. MIT Lincoln Lab heritage from LLCD. 130x improvement over S-band radio. First crewed deep-space optical link.

Four international CubeSats deployed from Orion's stage adapter ring during HEO: TACHELES (Germany, electronics radiation), ATENEA (Argentina, shielding materials), K-RadCube (South Korea, dosimetry), SWC-1 (Saudi Arabia, space weather). First CubeSats beyond LEO with crew.

Beyond LEO, crew faces Van Allen belt transit (trapped protons/electrons), galactic cosmic rays (GCR, heavy ions at ~1 GeV/nucleon), and solar energetic particles (SEPs). HERA active dosimeter and Timepix sensors provide real-time monitoring. First crewed exposure since Apollo 17.

54-year gap between Apollo 17 (1972) and Artemis II (2026). Technology evolved from 74 KB guidance computers to multi-core flight systems, but the physics — orbital mechanics, delta-v budgets, re-entry heating — remains unchanged. Free-return trajectory geometry identical to Apollo 13.

Cognitive shift reported by astronauts upon viewing Earth from space. Heightened awareness of ecological interconnection, atmospheric fragility, and planetary unity. Artemis II crew will observe Earth from lunar distance — the farthest human perspective since Apollo.

CSA contribution spans from Canadarm (1981 Shuttle) through Canadarm2 (ISS) to Canadarm3 (Gateway). Jeremy Hansen becomes first Canadian beyond LEO. Gateway robotic arm secures Canadian astronaut lunar access. Heritage of robotics excellence enabling human exploration.
